text: This video shows how transperineal operative access can be used to assist laparoscopic completion protectomy and parastomal hernia repair in a male patient. The man had previously had an emergency total colectomy and end ileostomy for ulcerative colitis. He did not want an ilioanal pouch and his definitive completion operation begins now with laparoscopic mobilization of the residual rectal stump. most especially in its posterior and lateral aspect. The transperineal access will be used to mobilize the lower rectum, especially anteriorly.

The surgeon places a single port access device, the Gel Point Path by Applied Medical, into the perineal wound after suture closure of the anus. The suture-closed anorectum is visible, allowing circumferential mobilization. Point cautery is used to maintain haemostasis during this stage of the operation, facilitating the removal of the specimen via the perineal access.

The laparoscopic view shows the transperineal approach with visible gas bubbles indicating the connection between the dissection from below and above. The specimen is mobilized and removed, and the gel point path is reinserted into the perineal access, providing a clear view of the abdominal compartment. The end ileostomy is mobilized, and a dyna mesh is placed around the stoma, with the small bowel and mesh returned to the peritoneal cavity. An Alexa's wound protector is placed into the stoma incision site.

The surgeon places a mesh in a double layer, ensuring proper orientation and secure tacking against the abdominal wall. A glove is used to maintain a gas seal around the abdominal incision site, achieving a stable pneumoperitoneum. Two working instruments are visible, inserted through the transperitoneal single port access device, allowing perpendicular tacking of the mesh with digital indentation to confirm its position.

The surgeon places tucks circumferentially around the hernia repair site, using a right-angled approach facilitated by the transperineal access. This technique provides clear visualization of each tuck placement, contrasting with the oblique placement seen with conventional laparoscopic ports.

The surgeon irrigates the transperineal wound and pelvic cavity with Betadine. The subumbilical trocar access used for laparoscopic mobilization of the upper rectum is included in the mesh repair. After placing an initial row of tacks circumferentially, the wound protector is removed, allowing placement of an inner circle of tacks. The gel point path is then removed, and the perineal wound is closed in the usual manner.

There were no interoperative difficulties during the case and the patient had a normal post-operative course. He remains well now, twelve months after the operation and has had neither recurrence of his parastomal hernia nor any perineal wound healing complications. We've done this in an additional two patients also having barostoma hernia at the time of their completion proctectomy with no adverse complications in the intraoperative, early or intermediate postoperative.
